You are ONE of N parallel CANDIDATE planners (generate-N-then-judge, candidate mode) for goal **G7**. Read the goal item `fetch_item("goals", "G7")` and its linked defects (defects:D36, defects:D32-context, defects:D37) for the confirmed root cause + suggestedFix. Ground yourself READ-ONLY against the repo (internal/reseq/reseq.go admit/Rebaseline, internal/bind/multipath.go rq.Observe + SetPeerRemote→Rebaseline, internal/device/failover.go, the T38 session-epoch machinery, internal/device/device.go up()).

G7 is DEFECT-SEEDED (skip clarifying — the root cause is confirmed). Produce a candidate task-DAG that FIXES defects:D36 (+ folds in defects:D37): trigger Resequencer.Rebaseline() on a DETECTED PEER RESTART (via the authenticated T38 responder-challenge session-epoch change), wired into BOTH the edge single-concentrator path AND the concentrator per-peer path (both currently lack any Rebaseline trigger); AND drive a handshake (re)initiation off the first path StateUp transition (D37). Validate with a netns one-sided-restart e2e (privileged run DEFERRED to the o3 + llm-ubuntu-0 hosts, G2 pattern).

Return your candidate plan as a fenced ```json block ONLY (write NOTHING to any ledger), in the shape: { "milestones": [{ "title": "...", "dependsOn": [] }], "tasks": [{ "headline": "...", "description": "...", "acceptance": "...", "suggestedModel": "frontier|standard|fast", "milestone": "<milestone title>", "dependsOn": ["<task headline>"], "ledgerRefs": ["goals:G7", "defects:D36"] }], "rationale": "..." }. Keep the DAG tight and testable (surface→wiring→e2e splits where useful; unit tests before the deferred privileged e2e). Each fix task's ledgerRefs must include the defect(s) it resolves. End with a `### Session summary` block.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: {"item":{"id":"D36","milestoneId":"M28","status":"root-caused","fields":{"headline":"One-sided restart → multi-minute outage: peer holding the old WG session does not promptly re-handshake","severity":"high","description":"[fixes-doc D2, S1 — production deploy, confirmed repeatedly.] When EITHER end restarts, the peer still holding the old session does not promptly re-handshake; the tunnel stays down for MINUTES until a rekey timer fires. Restart edge → o3 stale → down for minutes; restart o3 → edge stale → down for minutes; only restarting BOTH ~together (both re-initiate from scratch) converges in ~25 s. For a WAN-failover product this is severe — any reboot of the Pi or the concentrator is a multi-minute client outage. NOTE the layer: this is the INNER WG session going stale, distinct from the already-resolved OUTER probe/liveness restart deadlock (defects:D12, fixed by the T38 responder-challenge session epoch) — outer paths recover, but the stale WG keypair on the surviving side is not superseded. Compounded by fixes-doc D3 (see the startup-handshake defect): the restarted side's first init fires before path liveness and is not aggressively retried.","suggestedFix":"Extend the trusted resequencer re-anchor (Rebaseline) from the hub-failover-only trigger to a detected PEER RESTART, on BOTH the edge non-failover path AND the concentrator role. The T38 responder-challenge session-epoch (which already authenticates and detects a peer's new session on the OUTER plane, fixing D12) is the natural trusted control event: when a peer's authenticated session epoch changes (restart), call the peer's Resequencer.Rebaseline() so the next inbound DATA frame (the wrapped WG init/response) re-anchors `next` instead of being SUSPECT-dropped. Wire it in the concentrator per-peer path and the edge single-concentrator path (both currently lack any Rebaseline trigger). VALIDATE with a netns one-sided-restart e2e (deferred to the o3 + llm-ubuntu-0 hosts, G2 pattern): saturate the bond so the surviving resequencer's `next` advances past the window, then restart ONLY the edge (run A) and ONLY the concentrator (run B); assert reconvergence well under the WG rekey timeout (target ~= the ~25s both-ends-fresh baseline) and capture reseq dropSuspect/rebaseline counters + the wanbond_session_established 0→1 timestamp per direction. NOTE: static analysis predicts the edge-restart direction should recover in ~10s absent the drop, so the e2e also quantifies the true per-direction magnitude (field-reported as minutes).","sourceRefs":["wanbond-fixes.md §A D2","wanbond-fixes.md §C C5"],"ledgerRefs":["defects:D12"],"tags":["production-deploy","restart","re-handshake"],"rootCause":"CONFIRMED (H2). The multi-minute one-sided-restart outage is the OUTER-plane per-peer resequencer dropping the restarted peer's low-outer-seq frames as SUSPECT — the SAME mechanism as the hardware-root-caused D32, but on the one-sided-restart path which has no rescue trigger. Every inner WG datagram (including the opaque handshake initiation/response) is wrapped in an outer DATA frame and pushed through the peer's resequencer (internal/bind/multipath.go:1619-1650 rq.Observe). After a peer restarts, its outer-seq resets to ~1, far below the stale-high release point `next` the pre-restart high-rate stream advanced the surviving side's resequencer to; admit() (internal/reseq/reseq.go:285-297) treats any frame >1 window below `next` as SUSPECT and drops it (dropSuspect++) unless the unauthenticated tryResync corroborates 3 distinct low seqs within one window — which a freshly re-handshaking peer emitting ~1 DATA frame per RekeyTimeout cannot achieve in time (reseq.go:529-546, the Rebaseline doc naming exactly this as D32). The Rebaseline() trusted re-anchor that defeats this is wired to EXACTLY ONE caller, SetPeerRemote (multipath.go:2167-2182), whose only callers are the edge hub-failover controller — so a plain one-sided EDGE restart (live paths, no endpoint change) never triggers it, and the CONCENTRATOR role runs no failover at all (failover.go:496 noop), so neither side re-anchors on a plain restart. RULED OUT: a WG-layer supersede failure (H2(a)) — amneziawg-go's responder consumes+responds to a fresh init immediately (receive.go:399-417, gated only by tai64n + a 20ms flood window), so the stall is NOT the engine holding the old keypair; the init simply never reaches the engine because the resequencer drops it. COMPOUNDING: the restarted side's first init fires pre-liveness (D37) and is not re-driven off the first path-up edge, but WG's 5s retransmit bounds that to seconds.","sessionLogs":[".cq/logs/20260714-090009-a8d87a208742a8de8.md"],"rawLogs":[".cq/logs/raw/20260714-090009-a8d87a208742a8de8.jsonl"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-13T22:48:32.358Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T09:00:55.833Z","author":"fable-5","session":"671d5adc-7e2a-440e-b87d-6da40edeb7b7"}}

RULED OUT: a WG-layer supersede failure (H2(a)) — amneziawg-go's responder consumes+responds to a fresh init immediately (receive.go:399-417, gated only by tai64n + a 20ms flood window), so the stall is NOT the engine holding the old keypair; the init simply never reaches the engine because the resequencer drops it. COMPOUNDING: the restarted side's first init fires pre-liveness (D37) and is not re-driven off the first path-up edge, but WG's 5s retransmit bounds that to seconds.","sessionLogs":[".cq/logs/20260714-090009-a8d87a208742a8de8.md"],"rawLogs":[".cq/logs/raw/20260714-090009-a8d87a208742a8de8.jsonl"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-13T22:48:32.358Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T09:00:55.833Z","author":"fable-5","session":"671d5adc-7e2a-440e-b87d-6da40edeb7b7"}}

content.text: {"item":{"id":"D37","milestoneId":"M28","status":"root-caused","fields":{"headline":"Startup handshake fires before path liveness and is not re-driven off the first path-up edge","severity":"medium","description":"[fixes-doc D3, S2 — production deploy.] On every start the edge logs one `Failed to send handshake initiation: bind: no healthy path with a known remote endpoint` (paths not up yet); paths transition Up ~0.6 s later — and then nothing: the first init is wasted and re-initiation is not visibly driven off the path-up edge, leaving the tunnel waiting on WG's own retransmit timers. Compounds the one-sided-restart outage (defects:D36). WG should (re)initiate the moment the first path becomes live. Related observability: the same startup window logs `no healthy path` at ERROR during the normal ~1 s liveness warmup (fixes-doc I4 — downgrade/rate-limit it; tracked in the wanbond-fixes goal).","suggestedFix":"Drive a handshake (re)initiation off the FIRST path StateUp transition (the telemetry liveness edge → poke the engine to SendHandshakeInitiation / expire-and-retry), rather than relying on the engine's own back-off. FOLDED INTO the D36 fix goal G7 (same startup/restart handshake-robustness surface — both wire a trusted control event into the handshake/resequencer path). Validate in the same netns e2e: from cold start, time-to-first-handshake tracks path-up (+~1 RTT), not a 5s retransmit timer. The related ERROR-spam downgrade (I4) shipped separately via the engineLogger warmup coalescing (T103).","sourceRefs":["wanbond-fixes.md §A D3","wanbond-fixes.md §B I4"],"ledgerRefs":["defects:D36"],"tags":["production-deploy","startup","re-handshake"],"rootCause":"CONFIRMED (established by the D36/H2 investigation, evidence item 9). The edge's first WG handshake initiation fires before path liveness (every path starts Down; the bind returns ErrNoHealthyPath, 'no healthy path with a known remote endpoint') and is NOT re-driven when the first path reaches StateUp ~0.6s later: internal/device/device.go up() (:258-437) issues NO boot-time forced SendHandshakeInitiation, and startFailoverAndResolution returns a noop for a single-concentrator edge (internal/device/failover.go), so nothing pokes the engine off the liveness edge — the tunnel then waits on amneziawg-go's own RekeyTimeout (5s) retransmit back-off (timers.go:99-107). Confirmed no wanbond hook re-initiates on path-up. This is the compounding startup half of the D36 one-sided-restart outage (the restart half being the resequencer SUSPECT-drop, defects:D36).","sessionLogs":[".cq/logs/20260714-090009-a8d87a208742a8de8.md"],"rawLogs":[".cq/logs/raw/20260714-090009-a8d87a208742a8de8.jsonl"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-13T22:48:44.390Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T09:05:20.532Z","author":"fable-5","session":"671d5adc-7e2a-440e-b87d-6da40edeb7b7"}}

content.text: {"item":{"id":"D32","milestoneId":"M15","status":"resolved","fields":{"headline":"Hub failover: switch decision + outer PROBE liveness recover to standby hub#2, but inner WG data traffic does NOT resume via the standby within the window (hardware e2e)","severity":"high","description":"Found by T62 hardware e2e on llm-ubuntu-0 (amd64), commit 5ffc411 (main + T62 test; T57 hub-failover feature merged). TestHubFailoverStandbySwitch FAILS deterministically 3/3 clean-setup runs at hub_failover_test.go:150 ('tunnel did not resume via standby hub#2 within 10.2s of the hub#1 outage'). The observed sequence after hub#1 is killed (its bridge port down = L2 blackhole): (1) edge detects all-paths-down (path liveness up->down, scheduler no eligible path); (2) edge LOGS the 'hub failover:' WARN naming hub#2 (from_index:0 to_index:1 to_endpoint:'10.100.0.3:51820' endpoints:2) — the T57 controller advances + repoints as designed; (3) OUTER path liveness RECOVERS to UP on hub#2 ~2s after the kill (path liveness uplink down->up; scheduler active path change reason:failover) and stays up, no flapping; (4) YET base.pingUntil(concInner) — inner tunnel traffic, which can ONLY be carried by hub#2 since hub#1 is blackholed — NEVER succeeds for the remaining ~8s of the 10.2s window. So the outer PROBE/liveness plane repoints to hub#2 and the T57 re-handshake fires, but INNER encrypted WG data does not traverse to hub#2. HUB_FAILOVER_RESUME_MS never emitted (success-path-only). The single-endpoint guard PASSES (no spurious switch). Test topology is correctly configured (verified statically): both hubs run full concentrator daemons with the IDENTICAL WG private key and the edge as an allowed peer (allowed_ips=concInner); edge peer has endpoints=[hub1,hub2], public_key=hubPub. So this is NOT a hub-misconfiguration; the defect is in the DATA PLANE after SetPeerRemote/ExpireCurrentKeypairs+SendHandshakeInitiation. Hypothesis to confirm on hardware: the WG handshake to hub#2 does not COMPLETE (hub#2 never establishes the session) OR completes but data does not forward — hub daemon logs are suppressed (config level='error') so this needs an instrumented hardware re-run (hub#2 at level info/debug + tcpdump/counters) to determine whether hub#2 receives+completes the edge's re-handshake. This validates that T57's unit tests (mocked liveness + recordingRemote) proved the CONTROLLER LOGIC but not the actual end-to-end data re-establishment; the e2e caught the gap. Q18 (multi-concentrator hub failover) is IN-SCOPE for the pilot, so this must work.","ledgerRefs":["tasks:T57","tasks:T62","goals:G2"],"rootCause":"CONFIRMED by instrumented hardware reproduction (llm-ubuntu-0, edge+hub debug logs + tcpdump). Verdict (d): neither edge re-handshake timing (H-A) nor concentrator session establishment (H-B). The WG re-handshake COMPLETES ON THE WIRE IN BOTH DIRECTIONS: at T0+6.89s the edge's WG retransmit (try 2) egresses an initiation that REACHES hub#2 (wire: 10.100.0.1>10.100.0.3 len188), hub#2 logs 'Received handshake initiation' and 'Sending handshake response', and the response EGRESSES (wire: 10.100.0.3>10.100.0.1 len132). But the edge NEVER logs 'Received handshake response' -> keypair never established -> inner ping never succeeds. BREAKING STAGE: the edge's shared receive RESEQUENCER (internal/reseq/reseq.go admit()) DROPS hub#2's handshake-response DATA frame. WG payloads ride wanbond DATA frames tagged with the SENDER's per-bond outer-seq and are reordered by ONE shared reseq.Resequencer before the engine (PROBES are a different frame kind and bypass the resequencer -- which is exactly why OUTER liveness recovers while inner data does not). During the pre-kill iperf3 baseline hub#1 sent >>2048 DATA frames, advancing the resequencer release point `next` far above resequencerWindow (2048). Hub#2 is a SEPARATE process; its outer-seq starts near 1, so its response frame carries seq~1. In admit(): seq<next AND next-seq>window -> the SUSPECT branch; tryResync needs resyncCorroborate=3 distinct low seqs within one window to re-pin `next` backward, but only ONE hub#2 DATA frame arrives within the 10.2s window -> a lone frame only anchors the run, returns false -> dropSuspect++, dropped. Corroboration would need >=3 WG retransmits ~15s apart (past the window) -> deterministic permanent drop. The reseq.go comment names this exact case ('a peer restart resets outerSeq to 1, so its frames land here'). SetPeerRemote (multipath.go:1371) repoints path remotes ONLY -- it does NOT reset the resequencer, and NO Close/Open occurs on failover, so hub#1's high-water `next` persists across the concentrator switch. H-A (drop-at-switch, T0+1.6s 'Failed to send handshake initiation: no healthy path') is REAL but non-fatal (WG retries and egresses at +6.9s). H-B refuted (hub#2 both receives and responds).","suggestedFix":"The defect: a hub failover changes the DATA-frame SENDER IDENTITY (hub#1->hub#2, whose outer-seq restarts near 1) WITHOUT re-baselining the shared receive resequencer's release point `next`. Fix in internal/reseq + the failover path. Preferred (minimal): on the SetPeerRemote hub switch, RESET/re-pin the reseq.Resequencer so the standby's fresh low outer-seq is accepted as the new baseline (treat a concentrator switch as an explicit resync trigger -- we KNOW the sender changed, so re-anchor `next` proactively instead of waiting for the 3-frame tryResync corroboration that cannot complete within the failover window). Wire it: the hubFailover controller (device/failover.go) already calls mp.SetPeerRemote(next) on switch; give Multipath a way to reset its resequencer (m.resequencer.Load().Reset() or equivalent) invoked from the same switch action. Alternative (more invasive): key resequencing per remote/sender rather than one persistent per-bond `next`. Add a unit test in internal/reseq: with `next` advanced past the window, a Reset (or switch signal) followed by a low-seq frame must ADMIT it (not dropSuspect). Then combined hardware re-validation with D33 fix -> TestHubFailoverStandbySwitch passes. Host artifacts: /tmp/d32-edge.log /tmp/d32-hub1.log /tmp/d32-hub2.log on llm-ubuntu-0."},"createdAt":"2026-07-13T16:18:50.224Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-13T16:56:00.721Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"45fdce95-2af6-42cd-8ddd-0c9faabc56ef"}}

content.content:
1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/hex"
5		"fmt"
6		"net/netip"
7		"sync"
8		"time"
9	
10		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
11	
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/bind"
13		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
14		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/dnsresolve"
15		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
16		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
17	)
18	
19	// hubFailoverSettle is the dwell a newly-selected concentrator endpoint is given to
20	// prove itself LIVE before the controller may advance again. It exists to keep a
21	// STILL-DOWN reading — caused merely by echoes not having returned from the just-
22	// selected hub yet — from skipping straight past a healthy standby.
23	//
24	// It must comfortably exceed the liveness UP-recovery latency: a DOWN path comes UP
25	// after telemetry.DefaultUpSuccesses (3) probe echoes at telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval
26	// (200ms) ≈ 600ms once probes reach a reachable hub. At 3s the dwell clears that with a
27	// wide margin for jitter/RTT, and also bounds the re-advance cadence while a whole hub
28	// fleet is down to at most one switch (one fresh handshake) per 3s. Hub loss is a
29	// coarse, rare event (a concentrator down), distinct from the sub-second PER-PATH
30	// failover the schedulers own, so a switch latency on this scale is acceptable.
31	const hubFailoverSettle = 3 * time.Second
32	
33	// hubHealth is one path's liveness verdict, the read side of the T13 telemetry state
34	// machine (*telemetry.Prober satisfies it). The hub-failover controller reads exactly
35	// the same up/down verdict the schedulers select on, so "hub loss" is derived from the
36	// very liveness plane per-path failover already runs on — no second detector.
37	type hubHealth interface {
38		State() telemetry.PathState
39	}
40	
41	// peerRemote repoints the whole bond's wire remote at a new concentrator endpoint
42	// (*bind.Multipath satisfies it via SetPeerRemote). It is the action half of a hub
43	// switch on the DATA/PROBE plane; the re-handshake is separate (rehandshake).
44	type peerRemote interface {
45		SetPeerRemote(ap netip.AddrPort)
46	}
47	
48	// rehandshake drops the current WireGuard session and initiates a FRESH one against the
49	// peer's static key — no hub-to-hub state handoff. The device wires it to the engine
50	// peer (expire keypairs + send handshake initiation); a test injects a counter.
51	type rehandshake func()
52	
53	// hubFailover is the edge-side active-standby concentrator failover controller (Q18/T57).
54	// It watches the per-path liveness plane and, when EVERY path to the currently-active
55	// concentrator is DOWN simultaneously (HUB LOSS — distinct from a single path failing,
56	// which the schedulers already absorb), advances to the next endpoint in the ordered
57	// concentrator list, repoints every path's remote at it, and initiates a fresh WG
58	// re-handshake. It re-arms against the new endpoint and, if that one is also fully down,
59	// advances again.
60	//
61	// End-of-list policy: WRAP (round-robin modulo the list length). Once the last standby
62	// is exhausted the controller cycles back to index 0 and keeps retrying every endpoint
63	// in order. Wrap is chosen over stop because it preserves availability — a concentrator
64	// that RECOVERS earlier in the list is retried and settled on within one full cycle,
65	// whereas stopping at the last endpoint would strand the edge on a dead hub even after
66	// endpoint 0 came back. The settle dwell bounds the churn to one switch (one handshake)
67	// per hubFailoverSettle while the whole fleet is down, so the round-robin is a slow,
68	// bounded retry, not a storm.
69	//
70	// GUARD (must-hold invariant): a SINGLE-endpoint list takes NO failover action at all —
71	// check returns immediately, so the remote is never repointed and no re-handshake is
72	// initiated. A one-concentrator deployment therefore behaves EXACTLY as it did before
73	// T57 (the endpoints-list "list" is the one-element normalization of the legacy single
74	// endpoint form, per T54).
75	type hubFailover struct {
76		// specs is the ORDERED concentrator endpoint set, one failoverSpec per configured
77		// endpoint entry (config.Peer.EndpointSpecs), in TOML order. Each spec OWNS its
78		// current expansion ([]netip.AddrPort): a literal is a fixed single entry; a hostname
79		// is its latest resolved record set (empty until the resolver — wired by a later task —
80		// first calls updateResolution). The FLATTENED concatenation of every spec's expansion,
81		// in spec order, is the failover order; the flattened index is what `check` advances.
82		// The set is MUTABLE: updateResolution swaps a spec's expansion in place under mu.
83		specs []failoverSpec
84		// health is the per-path liveness, one entry per configured path. Immutable after
85		// construction (the prober set is fixed for the tunnel's life).
86		health []hubHealth
87	
88		remote      peerRemote
89		rehandshake rehandshake
90		// install populates the ENGINE peer's endpoint via the UAPI/IpcSet path (R70). It is used on
91		// the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH only — the boot-adoption of a peer that came up endpoint-less —
92		// because SetPeerRemote (remote) repoints the bind's per-path remotes but NEVER sets the engine
93		// peer's endpoint, so after an endpoint-less boot a bare rehandshake would have no endpoint to
94		// transmit to. It is a REQUIRED constructor collaborator (newHubFailoverFromSpecs) — the device
95		// wires it to deviceInstallEndpoint at construction, so a lost production wiring line is a
96		// compile error, never a silent degradation to SetPeerRemote. Subsequent re-resolves of an
97		// already-installed peer take the remote (SetPeerRemote) repoint path — the engine's virtual
98		// endpoint stays stable per A1; only the bind remotes move.
99		install func(netip.AddrPort)
100		clock   telemetry.Clock
101		// settle is the dwell a freshly-selected endpoint gets before another advance is
102		// allowed (hubFailoverSettle in production; a test injects its own).
103		settle time.Duration
104		log    log.Logger
105	
106		mu sync.Mutex
107		// activeSpec and activeAddr are the SPEC-SCOPED identity of the ACTIVE endpoint (R70):
108		// the pair (owning spec index, AddrPort) — NEVER a bare flattened index and NEVER a
109		// bare AddrPort. Because a hostname may legitimately re-resolve onto the SAME AddrPort
110		// as another spec's literal (T67's load-time duplicate check is textual host:port
111		// only), duplicate AddrPort VALUES may appear across different specs in the flattened
112		// list; a bare-value re-map could silently bind the active pointer to the wrong spec's
113		// entry, so the active endpoint is addressed by (specIdx, AddrPort). activeSpec == -1
114		// means no active entry yet (every spec's expansion is empty). Guarded by mu.
115		activeSpec int
116		activeAddr netip.AddrPort
117		// idx is the ACTIVE endpoint's position in the FLATTENED list, DERIVED from
118		// (activeSpec, activeAddr) and re-mapped after every mutation. It is a read-only cache
119		// for observation/logging; the source of truth is the spec-scoped identity above. -1
120		// when there is no active entry. Guarded by mu.
121		idx int
122		// lastSwitch is when the active endpoint was last (re)selected — initialized to
123		// construction time so endpoint 0 gets the SAME settle grace at boot that a switched
124		// endpoint gets, preventing a boot-time false failover while probers are still DOWN
125		// before the first echo returns. Guarded by mu.
126		lastSwitch time.Time
127	}
128	
129	// failoverSpec is one ordered endpoint entry together with its CURRENT expansion. A
130	// literal entry's expansion is the fixed single AddrPort; a hostname entry's expansion is
131	// its latest resolved record set (Q32 multi-record), swapped in place by updateResolution.
132	// The spec itself (config.EndpointSpec) is retained so the controller can tell a hostname
133	// spec — which may grow/shrink at runtime — from an immutable literal, e.g. when deciding
134	// whether the failover loop may ever have work to do.
135	type failoverSpec struct {
136		spec  config.EndpointSpec
137		addrs []netip.AddrPort
138	}
139	
140	// newHubFailover builds the controller over an ordered list of IP-LITERAL endpoints — the
141	// legacy all-literal form (each address becomes its own single-entry literal spec). It is
142	// a pure constructor (no goroutine, no engine dependency beyond the injected collaborators)
143	// so its decision logic is unit-testable against a fake clock, fake health, and fake
144	// remote/rehandshake.
145	func newHubFailover(endpoints []netip.AddrPort, health []hubHealth, remote peerRemote, rh rehandshake, clock telemetry.Clock, settle time.Duration, lg log.Logger) *hubFailover {
146		specs := make([]failoverSpec, len(endpoints))
147		for i, ap := range endpoints {
148			specs[i] = failoverSpec{spec: config.EndpointSpec{Addr: ap}, addrs: []netip.AddrPort{ap}}
149		}
150		// An all-literal controller starts with every spec's expansion non-empty, so activeSpec is set
151		// at construction and the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH (h.install in updateResolution) is never
152		// reached — it takes no engine-endpoint install. Pass a no-op for the required install collaborator.
153		return newHubFailoverFromSpecs(specs, health, remote, rh, func(netip.AddrPort) {}, clock, settle, lg)
154	}
155	
156	// newHubFailoverFromSpecs builds the controller over the ordered, spec-keyed endpoint set.
157	// The active endpoint is initialized to the FIRST flattened entry (spec order); when every
158	// spec's expansion is empty (e.g. a hostname-only peer before the resolver has run) there
159	// is no active entry yet (activeSpec == -1) and check takes no action until an expansion
160	// appears. Like newHubFailover it injects no goroutine or engine dependency. install is a
161	// REQUIRED collaborator (the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH, R70): making it a constructor
162	// parameter — rather than a field patched in afterwards — turns a lost production wiring line
163	// into a compile error instead of a silent degradation to SetPeerRemote.
164	func newHubFailoverFromSpecs(specs []failoverSpec, health []hubHealth, remote peerRemote, rh rehandshake, install func(netip.AddrPort), clock telemetry.Clock, settle time.Duration, lg log.Logger) *hubFailover {
165		h := &hubFailover{
166			specs:       specs,
167			health:      health,
168			remote:      remote,
169			rehandshake: rh,
170			install:     install,
171			clock:       clock,
172			settle:      settle,
173			lastSwitch:  clock.Now(),
174			log:         lg,
175		}
176		h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr = h.entryAtLocked(0)
177		h.idx = h.flatIndexLocked(h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr)
178		return h
179	}
180	
181	// flatLenLocked is the number of endpoints in the FLATTENED list (the sum of every spec's
182	// current expansion length). Caller holds mu.
183	func (h *hubFailover) flatLenLocked() int {
184		n := 0
185		for i := range h.specs {
186			n += len(h.specs[i].addrs)
187		}
188		return n
189	}
190	
191	// canFailoverLocked reports whether the controller could EVER take a failover action: the
192	// flattened list already holds >= 2 endpoints, or SOME spec is a hostname whose runtime
193	// resolution may yet grow it. An all-literal set with a single entry can never grow, so its
194	// loop is a pure no-op and need not run. Caller holds mu.
195	func (h *hubFailover) canFailoverLocked() bool {
196		if h.flatLenLocked() >= 2 {
197			return true
198		}
199		for i := range h.specs {
200			if h.specs[i].spec.IsName {
201				return true
202			}
203		}
204		return false
205	}
206	
207	// flatIndexLocked returns the position of (specIdx, addr) in the FLATTENED list, or -1 if
208	// no such entry exists. It matches BOTH the owning spec index AND the AddrPort — never the
209	// bare value — so a duplicate AddrPort contributed by a DIFFERENT spec is skipped (R70);
210	// within the owning spec the FIRST occurrence wins. Caller holds mu.
211	func (h *hubFailover) flatIndexLocked(specIdx int, addr netip.AddrPort) int {
212		flat := 0
213		for si := range h.specs {
214			for _, a := range h.specs[si].addrs {
215				if si == specIdx && a == addr {
216					return flat
217				}
218				flat++
219			}
220		}
221		return -1
222	}
223	
224	// entryAtLocked maps a FLATTENED index back to its (owning spec index, AddrPort), or
225	// (-1, zero) when flatIdx is out of range (including an empty flattened list). Caller holds mu.
226	func (h *hubFailover) entryAtLocked(flatIdx int) (int, netip.AddrPort) {
227		if flatIdx < 0 {
228			return -1, netip.AddrPort{}
229		}
230		n := 0
231		for si := range h.specs {
232			for _, a := range h.specs[si].addrs {
233				if n == flatIdx {
234					return si, a
235				}
236				n++
237			}
238		}
239		return -1, netip.AddrPort{}
240	}
241	
242	// containsAddrPort reports whether addrs holds target (exact AddrPort equality).
243	func containsAddrPort(addrs []netip.AddrPort, target netip.AddrPort) bool {
244		for _, a := range addrs {
245			if a == target {
246				return true
247			}
248		}
249		return false
250	}
251	
252	// updateResolution swaps specIdx's expansion under h.mu — the sole mutation point of the
253	// endpoint set — and reconciles the active pointer WITHIN its owning spec (R70 spec-scoped
254	// identity):
255	//   - BOOT ADOPTION (activeSpec == -1: every spec's expansion was empty, e.g. a hostname-
256	//     only peer before the resolver first ran): the first resolution that makes the
257	//     flattened list non-empty ADOPTS its head as the active endpoint — set (activeSpec,
258	//     activeAddr), point the bond via exactly ONE SetPeerRemote, one rehandshake, and ARM
259	//     the settle dwell (lastSwitch = now). Without this a single-hostname peer's bond would
260	//     never receive any endpoint: check cannot rescue it either, because a one-record
261	//     expansion keeps the flattened length at 1, permanently under check's total<2 guard.
262	//   - A STANDBY-only change (specIdx != activeSpec) NEVER touches the bond: the active
263	//     entry is untouched; only the DERIVED flattened idx is re-mapped (an earlier spec
264	//     growing/shrinking shifts the active entry's flattened position without moving it).
265	//   - When the ACTIVE spec's own expansion changes: if the active AddrPort SURVIVES the
266	//     swap within that spec, the active entry is stable — strictly NO repoint (Q31/D32
267	//     no-op suppression), only the idx re-maps. If the active AddrPort is GONE, the active
268	//     endpoint's IP has genuinely changed: repoint via exactly ONE SetPeerRemote (D32
269	//     disruptive → Rebaseline + rehandshake) and one rehandshake to the new expansion's
270	//     first entry, and RE-ARM the settle dwell (lastSwitch = now) so the freshly-repointed
271	//     endpoint gets its full grace before a subsequent all-down check may advance off it.
272	//
273	// A change that empties the active spec (nothing to point at) leaves the active identity
274	// stale — the flattened list no longer contains it — and the next check on hub loss
275	// advances off it; no repoint is issued to a non-existent endpoint.
276	func (h *hubFailover) updateResolution(specIdx int, addrs []netip.AddrPort) {
277		h.mu.Lock()
278		defer h.mu.Unlock()
279	
280		if specIdx < 0 || specIdx >= len(h.specs) {
281			return
282		}
283		h.specs[specIdx].addrs = addrs
284	
285		switch {
286		case h.activeSpec == -1:
287			// No active entry yet (every spec was empty at boot). If this resolution populated
288			// the flattened list, adopt its head, point the bond at it, and arm the settle dwell.
289			if adoptSpec, adoptAddr := h.entryAtLocked(0); adoptSpec != -1 {
290				h.activeSpec = adoptSpec
291				h.activeAddr = adoptAddr
292				h.lastSwitch = h.clock.Now()
293				// FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH (R70): the peer booted endpoint-less, so the engine peer
294				// has NO endpoint yet. INSTALL the resolved endpoint on the engine peer via the UAPI
295				// path (install) FIRST — a SetPeerRemote repoint would move the bind's per-path remotes
296				// but leave the engine peer endpoint-less, so the following rehandshake would have no
297				// endpoint to transmit to — THEN rehandshake, which now has an addressable endpoint.
298				h.install(adoptAddr)
299				if h.rehandshake != nil {
300					h.rehandshake()
301				}
302				h.log.Warn("hub failover: first endpoint resolution; installed active concentrator endpoint on the engine peer and re-handshaked",
303					"spec_index", adoptSpec, "to_endpoint", adoptAddr.String())
304			}
305		case specIdx == h.activeSpec:
306			if !containsAddrPort(addrs, h.activeAddr) && len(addrs) > 0 {
307				// The active endpoint's own spec re-resolved off the current AddrPort: its IP
308				// changed. Repoint the bond once, re-handshake against the new first entry, and
309				// re-arm the settle dwell against the freshly-repointed endpoint.
310				prev := h.activeAddr
311				h.activeAddr = addrs[0]
312				h.lastSwitch = h.clock.Now()
313				h.remote.SetPeerRemote(h.activeAddr)
314				if h.rehandshake != nil {
315					h.rehandshake()
316				}
317				h.log.Warn("hub failover: active concentrator endpoint re-resolved; repointed remote and re-handshaked",
318					"spec_index", specIdx, "from_endpoint", prev.String(), "to_endpoint", h.activeAddr.String())
319			}
320			// Survived (or empty expansion): no repoint. idx re-maps below.
321		}
322	
323		h.idx = h.flatIndexLocked(h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr)
324	}
325	
326	// allPathsDown reports HUB LOSS — every path to the active concentrator DOWN — under h.mu.
327	// It is the read half of the coordination surface the re-resolution controller (T73) shares
328	// with the failover controller (Q34): the resolver reads the SAME liveness sweep the failover
329	// loop advances on, so a liveness-loss out-of-band re-resolve and a hub-loss advance derive
330	// from one detector, never two that could disagree.
331	func (h *hubFailover) allPathsDown() bool {
332		h.mu.Lock()
333		defer h.mu.Unlock()
334		return h.allDownLocked()
335	}
336	
337	// activeSpecIndex returns the owning spec index of the currently-active endpoint under h.mu,
338	// or -1 when there is no active entry yet (every spec's expansion empty). The re-resolution
339	// controller (T73) reads it to know WHICH spec a liveness-loss trigger must re-resolve out of
340	// band; the identity is spec-scoped (R70), so this is the spec index, never a flattened index.
341	func (h *hubFailover) activeSpecIndex() int {
342		h.mu.Lock()
343		defer h.mu.Unlock()
344		return h.activeSpec
345	}
346	
347	// allDownLocked reports HUB LOSS: every path's liveness to the active concentrator is
348	// DOWN simultaneously. An empty health set is NOT hub loss (there is nothing to declare
349	// dead — a bind without the probe transport never drives this). Caller holds mu.
350	func (h *hubFailover) allDownLocked() bool {
351		if len(h.health) == 0 {
352			return false
353		}
354		for _, hp := range h.health {
355			if hp.State() != telemetry.StateDown {
356				return false
357			}
358		}
359		return true
360	}
361	
362	// check is one failover-evaluation step: on confirmed hub loss (and once the active
363	// endpoint's settle dwell has elapsed) it advances to the next endpoint, repoints the
364	// bond's remote, and initiates a fresh re-handshake. It is idempotent and cheap in the
365	// steady state (any path up, or a single-endpoint list) — a length check plus a liveness
366	// sweep — so a periodic loop may call it at the probe cadence.
367	func (h *hubFailover) check() {
368		h.mu.Lock()
369		defer h.mu.Unlock()
370	
371		// GUARD: a FLATTENED list of fewer than two endpoints takes NO failover action — no
372		// advance, no remote repoint, no re-handshake — so a one-concentrator deployment is
373		// byte-for-byte the pre-T57 behaviour, and a hostname peer whose resolution has not yet
374		// yielded a second endpoint waits rather than churning.
375		total := h.flatLenLocked()
376		if total < 2 {
377			return
378		}
379		if !h.allDownLocked() {
380			return
381		}
382		// Settle dwell: give the currently-active endpoint time for its liveness to recover
383		// before advancing, so a not-yet-echoed (transiently DOWN) healthy endpoint is not
384		// skipped. Applies at boot (endpoint 0) and after every switch.
385		now := h.clock.Now()
386		if now.Sub(h.lastSwitch) < h.settle {
387			return
388		}
389	
390		// Advance in FLATTENED order from the active entry's current flattened position. The
391		// position is re-derived from the spec-scoped identity every step, so a standby spec
392		// that grew/shrank since the last advance is walked at its new offset. A missing active
393		// (activeSpec == -1, or its AddrPort no longer present) resumes from the head.
394		prev := h.flatIndexLocked(h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr)
395		var nextIdx int
396		if prev < 0 {
397			nextIdx = 0
398		} else {
399			nextIdx = (prev + 1) % total
400		}
401		nextSpec, next := h.entryAtLocked(nextIdx)
402		h.activeSpec = nextSpec
403		h.activeAddr = next
404		h.idx = nextIdx
405		h.lastSwitch = now
406	
407		// Repoint the DATA/PROBE plane FIRST so probes immediately start re-arming detection
408		// against the new endpoint, then initiate the fresh WG re-handshake toward it.
409		h.remote.SetPeerRemote(next)
410		if h.rehandshake != nil {
411			h.rehandshake()
412		}
413		h.log.Warn("hub failover: all paths to active concentrator down; switched endpoint and re-handshaked",
414			"from_index", prev, "to_index", nextIdx, "to_spec", nextSpec, "to_endpoint", next.String(), "endpoints", total)
415	}
416	
417	// deviceRehandshake returns a rehandshake bound to the engine peer identified by pk: it
418	// EXPIRES the peer's current keypairs (dropping the old hub's session — a fresh session
419	// with NO hub-to-hub state handoff) and sends a fresh handshake initiation, which the
420	// Bind fans out to the just-repointed standby endpoint. All hubs in the ordered set
421	// share the peer's single WireGuard static key, so the SAME peer identity re-handshakes
422	// against whichever hub is now active. A peer the engine cannot resolve (never configured
423	// — impossible for the edge's sole peer) yields a no-op so the loop never dereferences
424	// nil. This is the ONLY engine-peer coupling the failover path takes; it stays in the
425	// device package alongside the rest of the engine wiring.
426	func deviceRehandshake(dev *awgdevice.Device, pk config.Key) rehandshake {
427		key := awgdevice.NoisePublicKey(pk.Bytes())
428		return func() {
429			peer := dev.LookupPeer(key)
430			if peer == nil {
431				return
432			}
433			// Drop the current session so the re-handshake starts a FRESH keypair (no hub-to-hub
434			// handoff); ExpireCurrentKeypairs also backdates lastSentHandshake so the immediately
435			// following initiation is not suppressed by the RekeyTimeout guard.
436			peer.ExpireCurrentKeypairs()
437			_ = peer.SendHandshakeInitiation(false)
438		}
439	}
440	
441	// deviceInstallEndpoint returns an install function that populates the ENGINE peer's endpoint via
442	// the UAPI/IpcSet path — an `endpoint=` line for pk routed through the engine to
443	// Multipath.ParseEndpoint (R70). This is the ONLY way to give the engine peer an addressable
444	// endpoint: SetPeerRemote repoints the bind's per-path remotes but leaves the engine peer's
445	// endpoint untouched, so after an endpoint-less tolerant boot a bare rehandshake has no endpoint
446	// to transmit to. The device installs the FIRST resolved endpoint through this before the first
447	// rehandshake; ParseEndpoint also seeds the (previously remoteless) per-path remotes with it, so
448	// the initiation egresses toward the resolved address. Re-issuing `public_key=` for an existing
449	// peer only updates its endpoint — it does not reset keys or allowed-ips. An IpcSet failure is
450	// logged; the loop retries on the next resolve. It stays in the device package alongside the rest
451	// of the engine wiring, mirroring deviceRehandshake.
452	func deviceInstallEndpoint(dev *awgdevice.Device, pk config.Key, lg log.Logger) func(netip.AddrPort) {
453		raw := pk.Bytes()
454		pubHex := hex.EncodeToString(raw[:])
455		return func(ap netip.AddrPort) {
456			if err := dev.IpcSet(fmt.Sprintf("public_key=%s\nendpoint=%s\n", pubHex, ap.String())); err != nil {
457				lg.Warn("hub failover: failed to install resolved endpoint on the engine peer",
458					"endpoint", ap.String(), "error", err.Error())
459			}
460		}
461	}
462	
463	// peerNeedsHubFailover reports whether a peer's endpoint set warrants a hub-failover
464	// controller: it does when the peer carries ANY hostname spec (its runtime resolution may
465	// yield further endpoints, and even a single hostname can re-resolve onto a new IP the bond
466	// must be repointed at) OR when >= 2 IP-literal endpoints are configured (an ordered
467	// active-standby list). A single-IP-literal peer needs NO controller — it is byte-for-byte
468	// the pre-G5 single-concentrator deployment (Q29), so the failover path never constructs
469	// or runs for it.
470	func peerNeedsHubFailover(peer config.Peer) bool {
471		for _, s := range peer.EndpointSpecs {
472			if s.IsName {
473				return true
474			}
475		}
476		return len(peer.Endpoints) >= 2
477	}
478	
479	// startFailoverAndResolution builds and starts the edge-side hub-failover monitor AND the
480	// re-resolution controller for cfg's concentrator peer, returning their two SEPARATE stoppers
481	// (device.Close invokes both). It returns no-op stoppers when hub failover does not apply: the
482	// concentrator role (it roams edges dynamically and has no endpoint list), a bind without the
483	// probe transport (no probers → no liveness plane), or no peer whose endpoint set warrants a
484	// controller (peerNeedsHubFailover — a single-IP-literal deployment gets none, staying
485	// behaviour-identical to pre-G5). The edge bonds every path to a SINGLE concentrator, so the whole
486	// per-path prober set IS the liveness of the paths to that concentrator (hub loss = every one
487	// DOWN); the controller drives the first qualifying peer.
488	//
489	// The controller's spec expansions come from boot (the bounded initial resolve, Q30): a hostname
490	// that resolved at boot starts with a non-empty expansion (activeSpec set, endpoint already
491	// installed at boot via the UAPI render), while one that did not resolve starts EMPTY (activeSpec
492	// == -1) and the re-resolution loop's first success adopts it through the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL
493	// PATH (ctrl.install, R70). The install collaborator is wired here to the engine peer.
494	func startFailoverAndResolution(cfg *config.Config, mp *bind.Multipath, probers []*telemetry.Prober, dev *awgdevice.Device, boot bootEndpoints, lg log.Logger) (stopFailover, stopResolution func()) {
495		noop := func() {}
496		if cfg.Role != config.RoleEdge || len(probers) == 0 {
497			return noop, noop
498		}
499		for i, peer := range cfg.WireGuard.Peers {
500			if !peerNeedsHubFailover(peer) {
501				continue
502			}
503			health := make([]hubHealth, len(probers))
504			for j, pr := range probers {
505				health[j] = pr
506			}
507			hlg := lg.Component("hubfailover")
508			// The engine-endpoint install used by the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH (R70) is a REQUIRED
509			// constructor collaborator: boot adoption of an endpoint-less peer installs the resolved
510			// endpoint on the engine peer through it, then rehandshakes. Passing it at construction
511			// (rather than patching a field afterwards) makes a lost wiring line a compile error, so it
512			// can never silently degrade to a SetPeerRemote that leaves the engine peer endpoint-less.
513			ctrl := newHubFailoverFromSpecs(
514				boot.specs[i], health, mp,
515				deviceRehandshake(dev, peer.PublicKey),
516				deviceInstallEndpoint(dev, peer.PublicKey, hlg),
517				telemetry.SystemClock{}, hubFailoverSettle,
518				hlg,
519			)
520			// Poll at the probe cadence: check is cheap (a length check plus a liveness sweep) and the
521			// settle dwell bounds actual switches, so a responsive poll only tightens detection latency
522			// without churning the remote.
523			stopFailover = ctrl.startHubFailoverLoop(telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval)
524			// Alongside failover, start the re-resolution controller (T73) over the SAME ctrl for a
525			// peer carrying hostname specs: it re-resolves each on the [dns] poll cadence and out-of-
526			// band on hub loss, feeding fresh records through ctrl.updateResolution (Q34 — the two
527			// coordinate purely through the shared lock and update API). An all-literal peer has no
528			// hostname to track, so it starts nothing.
529			stopResolution = startResolution(cfg, ctrl, peer, boot.resolver, lg)
530			return stopFailover, stopResolution
531		}
532		return noop, noop
533	}
534	
535	// startResolution builds and starts the re-resolution controller for peer's hostname endpoint
536	// specs over the already-constructed hub-failover controller, or returns a no-op stopper when the
537	// peer carries no hostname spec (an all-literal peer never re-resolves) or the resolver could not
538	// be constructed (nil — logged at boot). The resolver is the one built ONCE at boot from the
539	// (validated) [dns] block and shared with the bounded initial resolve, so hostname re-resolution
540	// never fails tunnel bring-up: hub failover itself is independent of re-resolution.
541	func startResolution(cfg *config.Config, ctrl *hubFailover, peer config.Peer, resolver dnsresolve.Resolver, lg log.Logger) func() {
542		targets := nameTargetsFromSpecs(peer.EndpointSpecs)
543		if len(targets) == 0 || resolver == nil {
544			return func() {}
545		}
546		res := newResolution(
547			resolver, ctrl, targets, pathFamiliesFromPaths(cfg.Paths),
548			telemetry.SystemClock{}, cfg.DNS.PollInterval, cfg.DNS.Timeout,
549			lg.Component("dnsresolve"),
550		)
551		// Drive step at the probe cadence: step is cheap in the steady state (a liveness read plus a
552		// clock compare) and gates the actual poll on the [dns] poll interval internally, so a
553		// responsive tick only tightens the liveness-loss re-resolve latency without over-resolving.
554		return res.startResolutionLoop(telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval)
555	}
556	
557	// startHubFailoverLoop launches the failover-evaluation goroutine: it calls check every
558	// interval until the returned stopper is invoked (idempotent). It mirrors the bind's
559	// StartProbeLoop/StartReconcileLoop lifecycle glue — a wall-clock ticker driving a
560	// decision function whose every timing choice runs through the injected Clock, so a test
561	// drives check directly against a fake clock and never starts this goroutine. It is a
562	// no-op (no-op stopper) for a controller that can NEVER fail over (canFailoverLocked: an
563	// all-literal set with fewer than two entries — no hostname to grow it) or a non-positive
564	// interval. A hostname peer whose expansion is still empty DOES start the loop: its
565	// resolution may later populate a second endpoint, and check no-ops cheaply until then.
566	func (h *hubFailover) startHubFailoverLoop(interval time.Duration) (stop func()) {
567		h.mu.Lock()
568		can := h.canFailoverLocked()
569		h.mu.Unlock()
570		if !can || interval <= 0 {
571			return func() {}
572		}
573		done := make(chan struct{})
574		go func() {
575			ticker := time.NewTicker(interval)
576			defer ticker.Stop()
577			for {
578				select {
579				case <-done:
580					return
581				case <-ticker.C:
582					h.check()
583				}
584			}
585		}()
586		var once sync.Once
587		return func() { once.Do(func() { close(done) }) }
588	}

110	// RecordEcho registers a successful, authenticated probe echo as a liveness
111	// heartbeat. Only echoes that are CONSECUTIVE — no more than DownAfter apart —
112	// accumulate toward Up; an echo that arrives after a longer silence starts the
113	// streak over, so UpAfterSuccesses stray echoes spread across arbitrary silence
114	// never bring a Down path Up. Once UpAfterSuccesses consecutive heartbeats
115	// accumulate, a Down path transitions Up.
116	func (l *Liveness) RecordEcho() {
117		now := l.clock.Now()
118		if l.haveGood && now.Sub(l.lastGood) > l.cfg.DownAfter {
119			l.goodStreak = 0 // silence broke the run of consecutive echoes
120		}
121		l.lastGood = now
122		l.haveGood = true
123		l.goodStreak++
124		if l.state == StateDown && l.goodStreak >= l.cfg.UpAfterSuccesses {
125			l.transition(StateUp, now)
126		}
127	}
128	
129	// Tick re-evaluates liveness against the clock. An Up path with no heartbeat for
130	// longer than DownAfter transitions Down; a Down path that has gone silent past
131	// DownAfter has its partial up-streak reset, so recovery requires a fresh run of
132	// UpAfterSuccesses consecutive echoes rather than echoes accumulated before the
133	// silence. Call Tick at least as often as the probe interval so detection latency
134	// stays within DownAfter plus one interval.
135	func (l *Liveness) Tick() {
136		now := l.clock.Now()
137		if !l.haveGood {
138			return
139		}
140		stale := now.Sub(l.lastGood) > l.cfg.DownAfter
141		switch {
142		case l.state == StateUp && stale:
143			l.goodStreak = 0
144			l.transition(StateDown, now)
145		case l.state == StateDown && stale:
146			l.goodStreak = 0
147		}
148	}
149

151	func (l *Liveness) State() PathState { return l.state }

154		from := l.state
155		l.state = to
156		l.log.Info("path liveness transition",
157			"from", from.String(),
158			"to", to.String(),
159			"silence_ms", now.Sub(l.lastGood).Milliseconds(),
160		)
161	}

That reflector is exactly the one on the *surviving* side, whose `pr.resequencer` SUSPECT-drops the restarted peer's low-outer-seq frames.
- Both roles construct `peerState.reflector` + `peerState.resequencer` (edge = single primary peer; concentrator = one per bound peer), and both flow through the SAME `Multipath.dispatchInbound` non-echo Probe branch (multipath.go:1689). So one wiring point — "on this peer's reflector adopting a new epoch, call `pr.resequencer.Load().Rebaseline()`" — covers BOTH the edge single-concentrator path and the concentrator per-peer path, symmetrically (edge-restart → concentrator rebaselines; concentrator-restart → edge rebaselines).
- `reseq.Rebaseline()` already exists and is D32-tested — no reseq change needed; the D36 fix is *detect + wire*.
- D37: `device.go up()` issues no forced init and the single-concentrator edge gets a noop from `startFailoverAndResolution`; the bind already flips a sticky `everUp` latch in `dispatchInbound` (multipath.go:1683) on first StateUp — the natural one-shot hook for a first-path-up rehandshake, mirroring `deviceRehandshake`.
